title: Xi Jinping Just Made It a Crime to Be Taiwanese
gist: The Diplomat's Kolas Yotaka argues that a week after Xi Jinping's September 2025 Tiananmen military parade, China's National People's Congress began deliberating a law that legally recasts Taiwanese people as PRC citizens (Article 21), pairing hard-power signaling with a lawfare push to absorb Taiwanese political identity.

themes.theme: Beijing's legal campaign on Taiwanese identity

insights.claim: Xi Jinping staged a military parade in Tiananmen Square on September 3, 2025, marking the 80th anniversary of China's WWII victory over Japan, featuring advanced weapons systems and attended by leaders of Russia and North Korea, in a deliberate display of authority to the military and legitimacy to the public.
insights.speaker: Kolas Yotaka
insights.so_what: Establishes the authoritarian-bloc show-of-force baseline against which Beijing's subsequent legal moves on Taiwan should be read; the parade lineup is a proxy for coordination among China, Russia, and North Korea.
insights.quote: a signal of authority to the military, a performance of legitimacy for the public
insights.locator: The display was deliberate in every detail

insights.claim: A week after the parade, the Standing Committee of China's National People's Congress began deliberating the 'Law of the People's Republic of China on the Promotion of Ethnic Unity,' which treats Taiwanese people as citizens of the PRC.
insights.speaker: Kolas Yotaka
insights.so_what: A concrete legal escalation beyond rhetoric: codifying Taiwanese people as PRC citizens lays the legal groundwork for treating Taiwanese political identity as a domestic matter, a material geopolitical risk factor for cross-strait tension.
insights.quote: The law treats Taiwanese people as citizens of the PRC.

insights.claim: Article 21 of the draft law mandates that the Chinese Communist Party work to 'strengthen the sense of belonging, identification, and honor of Taiwan compatriots toward the Chinese nation' and promote the idea that both sides of the strait 'belong to Chinese culture' and are both 'Chinese people.'
insights.speaker: Kolas Yotaka
insights.so_what: Provides the specific legal language Beijing could later invoke to treat Taiwanese identity or pro-independence expression as an 'ethnic disunity' offense — a template for lawfare against Taiwanese political identity worth monitoring for enforcement mechanisms.
insights.quote: strengthen the sense of belonging, identification, and honor of Taiwan compatriots toward the Chinese nation

insights.claim: By following a major military parade with a domestic law absorbing Taiwanese identity into PRC citizenship, Xi Jinping is combining hard-power signaling with legislative lawfare as a second, non-military weapon in his approach to Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Kolas Yotaka
insights.so_what: Signals Beijing may be pursuing a parallel 'legal annexation' track alongside military pressure on Taiwan — worth watching for how this law is enforced and whether it affects the cross-strait risk premium.
insights.quote: It was a signal of authority to the military, a performance of legitimacy for the public. But it was not his only weapon.
insights.locator: It was a signal of authority to the military
